static.description: Transform any legacy repository into an AI-agent-ready codebase by generating scaffolding files (AGENTS.md, CLAUDE.md, system_prompt.md, agent-context.json, memory/schema.md) using LLM-first analysis of actual code.
domain_concepts: agent-context.json: Machine-readable JSON file containing both static (user-editable) and dynamic (LLM-generated) metadata about a repository, used to ground AI agents
scaffolding files: The set of generated files (AGENTS.md, CLAUDE.md, system_prompt.md, agent-context.json, memory/schema.md) that give AI agents verified knowledge of a repository
context drift: When the generated scaffolding files become stale relative to actual code changes, detected weekly by a drift detection workflow
readiness score: A 100-point score measuring how well-prepared a codebase is for AI agent interaction
provider preset: A named configuration mapping three pipeline phases (analysis, generation, evaluation) to specific LLM model strings
Phase 1 — Collect: Mechanical file reading phase that gathers file tree, source files, config files, CI config, and README without any LLM reasoning
Phase 2 — Analyse: LLM-powered phase that reads collected code and produces structured JSON understanding of the repository
Phase 3 — Generate: LLM-powered phase that writes each output scaffolding file from scratch based on the analysis JSON
Phase 5 — Evaluate: Assessment phase that generates 15 questions across 5 categories and measures whether context files improve AI responses
restricted_write_paths: Paths that AI agents must never modify, identified during analysis and recorded in agent-context.json
MCP tool templates: Language-specific scaffold files (Python, Java, TypeScript, Go) for creating MCP-compatible tools

dynamic.architecture_summary: AgentReady follows a 5-phase pipeline: Phase 1 (Collect) reads file tree and source files mechanically, Phase 2 (Analyse) sends collected code to an LLM for structured JSON analysis, Phase 3 (Generate) uses an LLM to write scaffolding files, Phase 4 (Score) computes a readiness score, and Phase 5 (Evaluate) measures improvement via 15 questions. The system is provider-agnostic via LiteLLM, supporting Anthropic, OpenAI, Google, Groq, Mistral, Together, and Ollama with tiered model selection per phase (strongest for analysis, fastest for evaluation).

key_components.name: CLI
key_components.responsibility: Main entry point and argument parser; orchestrates the 5-phase pipeline, manages provider selection, and handles dry-run/eval-only/review modes

key_components.name: Analyser
key_components.responsibility: Phase 1 (Collect) and Phase 2 (Analyse) — reads repository files mechanically, then sends them to an LLM to produce structured analysis JSON

key_components.name: Generator
key_components.responsibility: Phase 3 (Generate) — takes analysis JSON and uses an LLM to write AGENTS.md, CLAUDE.md, system_prompt.md, agent-context.json, and memory/schema.md

key_components.name: Evaluator
key_components.responsibility: Phase 5 (Evaluate) — generates 15 questions across 5 categories (commands, safety, domain, architecture, pitfalls) and measures AI response quality with and without context

key_components.name: Reviewer
key_components.responsibility: PR review module — reviews pull requests using LLM reasoning grounded in agent-context.json, posts structured review comments via GitHub API

key_components.name: Tool Templates
key_components.responsibility: Language-specific MCP-compatible tool scaffolds for Python, Java, TypeScript, and Go

key_components.name: Install Workflow
key_components.responsibility: GitHub Actions workflow that pushes trigger workflows and issue templates into a target repository

key_components.name: Reusable Transformer
key_components.responsibility: Reusable GitHub Actions workflow that runs the full transformation pipeline on a target repo and opens a PR with generated files

key_components.name: Drift Detector
key_components.responsibility: Weekly scheduled workflow that detects meaningful drift between generated context files and actual codebase state

agent_safe_operations: Running ruff format --check for format verification
Running agent-ready with --dry-run flag to preview without writing files
Running agent-ready with --eval-only to evaluate existing context files
Reading any file under src/agent_ready/ for understanding
Adding new source files under src/agent_ready/
agent_forbidden_operations: Modifying .github/workflows/ CI files without explicit approval
Committing API keys or secrets to any file
Modifying the VERSION file directly (managed by release workflow)
Running shell commands with shell=True in subprocess calls (security boundary)
Editing files in src/agent_ready/templates/ without understanding template placeholder syntax
Modifying pyproject.toml version configuration (version is read from __init__.py via setuptools dynamic)
Pushing directly to main branch (all changes go through PRs)
potential_pitfalls: Zero hard dependencies — core package uses only stdlib; LLM features require installing with [ai] extra (pip install agent-ready[ai]) or litellm will ImportError
Version is managed dynamically via setuptools reading __version__ from src/agent_ready/__init__.py AND a separate VERSION file — both must stay in sync (release workflow handles this)
The analyser skips AgentReady-generated files (AGENTS.md, CLAUDE.md, agent-context.json, etc.) via SKIP_AGENT_FILES set — feeding them back would create circular analysis
Template files use double-brace placeholders like {{TOOL_NAME}} that look like Jinja2 but are NOT — they are simple string replacements; do not add Jinja2 logic
LLM API calls include retry logic with exponential backoff for 429/529/overloaded errors — tests mocking LLM calls must account for the retry wrapper
The ruff exclude configuration explicitly skips src/agent_ready/templates — template files intentionally contain placeholder syntax that would fail linting
PR reviewer treats all PR diff content as untrusted — it is sent to LLM APIs but never executed locally; subprocess calls use argument lists (no shell=True)
The CI workflow enforces minimum 50% code coverage (--cov-fail-under=50) — new code must maintain this threshold
Provider presets map three different models per provider (analysis/generation/evaluation) — using --model overrides ALL three phases with the same model
GitHub Actions workflows use secrets: inherit pattern — INSTALL_TOKEN and provider API keys must be set in the target repo's secrets, not just agent-ready's secrets
